The system is a medical inventory system.
The system tracks blood unit stock in a hospital blood bank.
The system manages compatibility between blood types.
The system monitors expiration.
The system raises alerts during shortages.
The system supports safe and timely transfusions.
The system tracks blood units by ABO type.
The system tracks blood units by Rh factor.
The system tracks blood units by collection date.
The system fixes expiry at 42 days after collection.
The system accepts transfusion requests.
Transfusion requests specify blood type.
Transfusion requests specify Rh factor.
Transfusion requests specify quantity.
The system matches requests to compatible units.
The system uses standard ABO/Rh donor compatibility for matching.
The system selects exact-type matches first.
The system selects closest expiry first.
The system reserves matched units.
The system releases a reservation back to stock if not confirmed as issued within 24 hours.
The system automatically expires units past their expiry date.
The system excludes expired units from matching.
The system raises a shortage alert whenever available stock of any blood type falls below 5 units.
The system shows a dashboard of stock per type.
The system shows a dashboard of units expiring within 7 days.
The system shows a dashboard of open requests.
The system acts as a centralized repository for monitoring and managing blood supply within a hospital transfusion service.
It automatically logs incoming blood units and updates available stock counts based on type and Rh factor.
A user can view real-time inventory levels and receive notifications when a specific blood type is low.
Using the tool results in a clear, up-to-date record of all blood products ready for transfusion.
The platform is an electronic ledger that keeps track of blood reserves and their attributes in a medical setting.
A unit is tracked by its ABO group, Rh status, and the date it was collected from the donor.
The system records each blood donation's type and Rh factor along with the collection timestamp.
Staff can enter collection details for a new blood bag, and the system immediately stores that information.
Recording a donation produces a permanent entry in the inventory with the unit's type and Rh marker.
The software logs every donated unit by its blood classification and the day it was drawn.
A transfusion request specifies the required blood group, Rh factor, and the number of units needed.
The system accepts a request that details the desired type, Rh, and volume for a patient.
A clinician can submit an order for a specific amount of blood of a certain type and Rh.
Submitting a request creates a pending demand in the system for the specified blood product.
A transfusion order is a demand for a particular blood type and Rh with a given unit count.
The matching algorithm first looks for exactly compatible units and then selects the one expiring soonest.
It pairs a request with a compatible blood unit by exact type match, then by nearest expiration date.
A user benefits because the system automatically finds the best available match without manual sorting.
Using the match feature results in the most suitable unit being assigned to the request efficiently.
The compatibility engine prioritizes identical type matches and then chooses the unit with the closest expiry.
A matched unit is held for a specific request and is automatically returned to stock if not confirmed within 24 hours.
The system reserves a unit for a request and releases it back if the transfusion is not finalized in a day.
Staff can trust that a reserved unit will be set aside, but will become available again if not used in time.
Reserving a unit creates a temporary hold that expires and releases the blood back into the general pool.
The reservation mechanism locks a unit for a request and automatically frees it after a 24-hour confirmation window.
Units past their expiration date are automatically marked as expired and removed from all matching searches.
The system ages out blood that is no longer viable and prevents it from being considered for transfusion.
Expired blood is automatically excluded from inventory counts and cannot be matched to any request.
This ensures that only fresh, non-expired units are ever presented as available for use.
The software automatically invalidates outdated units and removes them from the active supply pool.
A shortage alert is triggered whenever the available stock for any blood type drops below five units.
The system generates a warning when any blood type's inventory falls to fewer than five bags.
Users are notified immediately if a blood type becomes critically low, helping prevent transfusion delays.
The alert prompts action to restock or prioritize that blood type before a shortage becomes critical.
An automatic notification fires when the count of any blood type goes under the five-unit threshold.
The dashboard displays current stock levels for each type, units expiring within a week, and all open requests.
It shows a summary view of inventory by type, near-expiry items, and pending orders in one place.
Staff can see at a glance how many units of each type are on hand, which are about to expire, and what is needed.
The dashboard output gives a consolidated picture of supply status and upcoming demand.
The interface presents a combined overview of blood counts, soon-to-expire stock, and active requisitions.
